actions.thought: J4o is a very weak hand. Even being on the button in a 3-handed game, this hand has very low equity and playability. The best decision is to simply fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. T2o is a very weak hand to play out of position. While I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, this hand realization is poor and I'll likely face difficulties post-flop. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Queen-Trey offsuit. While I have positional advantage, this hand is extremely weak and does not warrant an open or a call against the blinds in the early stages of the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the small blind with 7-2 offsuit, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. While I have already invested 50 chips, calling to see the flop with such a weak hand out of position against the Big Blind is a negative expected value play. Preserving my stack early in the tournament is more important than chasing this pot.

With Ace-Four offsuit on the button in a three-handed game, this is a standard opening situation. Raising to 2.5x the big blind allows me to take initiative, pressure the blinds, and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play in position with a strong ace.

Pocket Tens is a very strong starting hand, especially 3-handed, and fares extremely well against a Button opening range. Flat calling from the Small Blind out of position invites the Big Blind to overcall or squeeze, and playing TT multi-way out of position on overcard boards is difficult. 3-betting to a standard out-of-position size (around 3.5x to 4x the open, so 900 chips) seizes the initiative, extracts value, and puts maximum pressure on the opponent.

I have Ace-Seven suited in the Big Blind. The Button opened to 250 and the Small Blind 3-bet to 900. In a 3-handed game, Ah7h is a strong hand with good playability and nut potential. Calling the 3-bet allows me to see a flop and potentially outplay my opponents post-flop or hit a strong draw. Folding is too tight for this hand strength, and 4-betting might over-leverage my hand this early in the tournament.

I opened the action to 250 and was immediately faced with a 3-bet to 900 and a flat call from my opponents. My Ace-Four offsuit is weak in this multi-way scenario and is likely dominated by the range of the 3-bettor, which typically includes strong pocket pairs or high-ranking cards. Calling the additional 650 into a bloated pot with this marginal holding is negative expected value, so I will fold to maintain my stack for a more favorable situation.

I flopped middle set on a draw-heavy board (Kc Tc 2d). Flash cold-called my pre-flop 3-bet from the Big Blind, indicating a strong, condensed range heavy in hands like AK, KQ, JJ-QQ, and strong suited connectors or flush draws. Since I am out of position and the board is wet, I need to bet to build the pot for a stack-off, extract value from top pair and draws, and deny free cards. A bet of roughly half to 60% of the pot (~1100 chips) accomplishes these goals nicely while keeping his drawing and one-pair hands in the pot, setting up well for turn and river sizing.

The flop of Tc-2d-Kc heavily favors the pre-flop 3-bettor's (Pro) range, which contains many strong Kings (AK, KQ), Tens (TT), and overpairs (AA, QQ, JJ). Having missed the flop completely with Ah-7h, I only have Ace-high and a weak backdoor flush draw. Pro's lead of 1100 into a 2050 pot represents significant strength. There is no reason to continue with this hand as I am likely drawing thin and would be forced to fold to further aggression on later streets. Preserving my stack for better spots is the most prudent strategy here.

I am on the Button with 85 suited. In a 3-handed game, while we can open a wide range from the Button, an 8-high two-gapper is generally too weak to justify an open-raise. I have a healthy stack and do not need to force action with sub-optimal hands, so folding is the standard and safest play.

It is folded to me in the Small Blind. J7o is a weak hand to play out of position against the Big Blind. While a raise might occasionally steal the blinds, J7o doesn't have much post-flop playability if called. Given it is early in the tournament and I want to avoid unnecessary variance with marginal hands out of position, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with a medium pocket pair (8h 8c). In a 3-handed game, this is a very strong starting hand. Raising is the standard play here to exert pressure on the blinds, build a pot, and capitalize on my positional advantage. A 3x open (300 chips) is a standard sizing to discourage the blinds from defending wide while still being committed enough to see a flop.

I am in the small blind with a weak suited hand (T2s). Calling from out of position against a button raise is generally suboptimal as the hand lacks strength and playability against a wider button range. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a more favorable situation rather than engaging with a marginal hand that will be difficult to navigate post-flop.

A6s is a strong enough hand to defend from the Big Blind against a Button open. While a 3-bet is a viable option to apply pressure, Flash showed some pre-flop stickiness in Hand 3. Calling keeps his opening range wide, maintains a manageable pot size while out of position, and allows us to realize our equity with our suited ace's post-flop playability.

We missed the flop completely, holding only Ace-high with weak backdoor possibilities and no flush draw. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard and safest play is to check our entire range to the raiser. This allows us to evaluate Flash's continuation bet and proceed accordingly. Given our weak holding on this board, we are preparing to fold if facing aggression.

I have a medium pocket pair (88) on a board with one overcard (Q). Since the Big Blind checked, I'll place a continuation bet to protect my hand against overcards and draws, and to extract value from weaker hands like smaller pairs or diamond draws. A bet of 300 into a 650 pot allows me to maintain initiative while keeping the pot manageable.

I missed the flop entirely, holding only Ace-high with no direct straight or flush draws. Facing a continuation bet from the pre-flop raiser while out of position, I do not have sufficient equity to continue. The most profitable play here is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

7h-5c is a bottom-tier starting hand with no high-card value, suitedness, or connectivity. It is a clear fold in almost any position. As I am in the early stages of the tournament, maintaining stack discipline and waiting for premium or playable hands is the optimal strategy.

T8 offsuit is a weak hand to play from the Small Blind. Being out of position post-flop against the Big Blind with a marginal holding will lead to difficult and unprofitable situations. The optimal decision is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

Holding Ace-Queen offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game is a premium starting hand. I want to open with a raise to build the pot, take control of the hand, and potentially win the blinds right away. A standard raise size is 2.5x the big blind.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h 8c, which is a weak offsuit hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Defending this hand out of position is generally unprofitable, especially against a solid opponent. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

J6o is a weak, non-connected hand that performs poorly out of position. Even though I am getting decent pot odds to call, playing a weak range against a Button open from the Big Blind is generally negative expected value. Given the tournament length of 150 hands, there is no need to play marginal hands OOP. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with A8s, a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. Raising is the standard play here to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the blinds. I will open for 3x the big blind.

A2 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive button open. Calling would require playing the rest of the hand without initiative and at a significant positional disadvantage, which is negative expected value. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

I am holding an offsuit Ten-Five (T5o) in the Big Blind. Flash has made a 3x open raise from the Button. T5o is a very weak starting hand with virtually no playability post-flop, making it a clear fold against any raise.

I am in position as the button with Q5 suited in a 3-handed game. This hand is well within my opening range, allowing me to exercise positional advantage and potentially take down the blinds. A raise to 250 is a standard sizing to accomplish this and build the pot.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Three offsuit facing a button raise from Flashlight. A3o is a weak, disconnected hand that plays poorly out of position. While the button may be opening a wide range, calling here will often lead to difficult post-flop spots, and 3-bet bluffing is unnecessary with my current stack depth and lack of specific reads. Folding is the standard and safest play.

In the Big Blind, I am facing a standard 2.5x open (250) from the Button (Flashlight). The Small Blind (Pro) has folded. I need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 550. This gives me pot odds of approximately 2.6:1. King-Six offsuit (K6o) is a strong enough hand to defend in a 3-handed game against a wide Button opening range. While I will be out of position post-flop, folding here would be too exploitable. Calling is the standard play to realize equity with this hand strength.

I have middle pair (Sixes) with a King kicker on a board of J-7-6 with two spades. This is a respectable hand but vulnerable to many overcards and draws. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight), the standard play is to check to the raiser to see how they react. Leading into the Button here is unnecessary and exposes me to a raise. I will check and likely call a standard-sized continuation bet to see a turn, as middle pair is too strong to fold immediately in a three-handed game.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and am in position with Queen-high on a board of 6-7-J. Although I have missed the flop, checking back offers no value and concedes control. A continuation bet is the standard play here to represent strength and potentially win the pot immediately. I will bet approximately half the pot (275) to exert pressure.

I have middle pair (sixes) with a King kicker. The board is Jack-Seven-Six with two spades. The Button (Flashlight) made a standard half-pot continuation bet of 275 into 550. In a three-handed game, middle pair is a strong enough hand to defend against a wide Button opening range. I am getting 3:1 pot odds, requiring approximately 25% equity to make calling profitable. Since my hand likely has significantly more equity than that against a standard c-betting range, I will call to see a turn card. Raising would overplay my hand, and folding would be too conservative given the pot odds and current hand strength.

On the turn card 4h, which is relatively blank and does not significantly alter the board's texture, I still hold middle pair (Sixes) with a King kicker. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor and have a hand with decent showdown value but not enough strength to bet for value, the most prudent action is to check. This allows me to see how the Button (Flashlight) reacts. If they check back, I get a free river. If they bet, I can evaluate the sizing to decide whether to continue based on pot odds and hand strength.

I am on the turn with Queen-high on a board of 6c-7s-Js-4h. My opponent check-called the flop, suggesting they have at least a piece of the board or a straight draw. Continuing to bluff here with only Queen-high is unlikely to be profitable against a player who already demonstrated interest in the pot on a coordinated board. Checking allows me to control the pot size and realize my showdown value, or potentially take a free card to the river if I am still behind.

On the river, the Queen of Spades is an overcard to my pair of sixes and completes the potential spade flush. Since I am out of position and my hand has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value, checking is the most appropriate action. Flashlight checked back on the turn, which often indicates a marginal hand or a draw. If they check back again, I may win against missed draws or weaker holdings. If they bet, I will have to evaluate their sizing and the likelihood of a bluff versus a completed flush or a Queen.

The board has become quite coordinated with the Queen of Spades completing a potential flush draw on the river. Holding top pair (Queens) with a weak kicker, my hand is essentially a marginal showdown hand. Since the opponent checked to me, betting into a board where a flush or straight is possible is risky, as I would likely only be called by hands that beat me or check-raised by a monster. Checking behind is the optimal play to realize my showdown value and avoid inflating a pot I may not win.
I am on the button in a 3-handed game with 86s. This is a reasonable suited one-gapper to open-raise with to attempt to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind.

J9o is a marginal hand that performs poorly out of position against a button open. Although I am getting 3:1 odds to call, the positional disadvantage and the risk of the big blind squeezing make folding the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Qc-4d is a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability, especially out of position against a button raise. Defending the big blind with this holding would be negative expected value. Given the stack depths and the early stage of the tournament, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Ace-Four offsuit. In three-handed play, Ace-high hands are generally strong enough to open for a raise from the button to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. A standard opening size of 2.5x to 3x the big blind is appropriate here to narrow the field or win the blinds immediately.

I hold 7-2 offsuit, which is widely recognized as the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Investing chips into a pot with this hand is negative expected value, especially since I am out of position. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision to preserve my stack.
2c 7c is too weak to call a raise, even from the big blind against a button open. I'll simply fold and wait for a better spot.

Ace-Eight offsuit is a solid starting hand for the button position. By raising to 250, I aim to steal the blinds or take the lead in the hand with a positional advantage. This is a standard aggressive play in this scenario to maximize equity and take control of the pot.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 6d, a suited one-gapper. Facing a raise from the Button, calling here would put me out of position for the rest of the hand against the pre-flop aggressor, with the Big Blind still left to act. This is a marginal holding that plays poorly out of position in a raised pot, so the most profitable long-term play is to simply fold.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Four offsuit. Flashlight opened from the Button for 250, and Pro folded the Small Blind. I need to call 150 more into a pot of 400, giving me pot odds of 3.67 to 1 (requiring about 21.4% equity). Against a standard Button opening range, K4o has enough equity to defend the blind, even though the hand plays poorly out of position. I will call and see a flop.

The flop is very dry (3h 2d 2s), and I am out of position as the Big Blind. Standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) to see how they react. Leading out (donk betting) doesn't make much sense on this board with King-high. I will check and evaluate based on Flashlight's action.

The board is extremely dry (3h-2d-2s) and likely missed both ranges, giving me an advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. With Ace-high in position, a continuation bet is standard to put pressure on the opponent and potentially take down the pot immediately, as it is unlikely the opponent connected with this board texture.

The flop is 3h 2d 2s, an extremely dry and paired board that likely missed most of Flashlight's button opening range. Flashlight's bet of 250 into a 550 pot is a standard continuation bet. My King-high (Ks 4c) has significant showdown value on this specific board and may even be the best hand currently. Given the pot odds of 3.2 to 1, I only need about 24% equity to justify a call. Since I have overcards and the board is so disconnected, I will call one street to see the turn and re-evaluate.

The turn card is the 7 of clubs, which is a total brick and doesn't change the board texture significantly. I have King-high and have already called a flop continuation bet. As the player out of position, I should continue to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out here (donk betting) doesn't make sense given the board doesn't hit my perceived big blind defending range particularly well, and I want to see how Flashlight reacts to the turn. I will check and evaluate whether to call or fold if Flashlight bets again.

The board remains very dry with the turn 7c. My opponent called a continuation bet on the flop, which indicates they have at least a piece of this board (likely a 2, a 3, or a pocket pair). My Ace-high has decent showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value here. Betting again would be turning my hand into a bluff, which is unnecessary against a calling range that has shown interest. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and see the river for free, minimizing risk while maintaining my potential to win at showdown if the opponent is also holding overcards or a low pair.

The board has double-paired (2-2 and 7-7), meaning the best five-card hand for most players will be the two pair on the board with the highest kicker in their hand. My hand is currently 7-7-2-2-K. I lose to any hand containing an Ace (7-7-2-2-A) and any hand that makes a full house (any 3, 2, or 7). Flashlight checked the turn, which suggests they might have a mediocre hand like Ace-high or a small pocket pair. Since I have some showdown value with a King kicker but cannot reasonably expect to fold out better hands by betting, checking is the most logical play. If Flashlight bets, I will likely fold as King-high is rarely good if the opponent shows aggression on this final street.

The board is paired with 2s and 7s, resulting in a 7-7-2-2-A board. My Ace-high effectively plays the board's two pairs with an Ace kicker. Any pocket pair or card higher than a 2 that the opponent holds would form a better two-pair or a stronger hand. Given the opponent called my continuation bet on the flop, they likely have at least a piece of the board or a pocket pair. Betting here would be a bluff that is unlikely to succeed against a calling range, while checking allows me to safely showdown and potentially chop or win if the opponent is also playing the board.
KQs is a premium hand 3-handed and I am on the button. I will raise to 2.5x to build the pot, take control of the hand, and potentially win the blinds right here.

I am in the Small Blind holding K6o. The button (Pro) has raised to 250. K6o is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raise, especially with the Big Blind still to act. While the pot odds are 2:1, the lack of playability and positional disadvantage make this a clear fold to preserve my stack for better spots.

Jack-six offsuit is a weak hand, especially out of position in the Big Blind against a button raise. The pot odds of 27% are not sufficient to justify calling with such low-equity cards. It is better to conserve chips and wait for a stronger hand or a more favorable position.

I am on the Button with Ace-King offsuit (AhKc), which is a premium starting hand. In a 3-handed game, this hand is extremely strong. My standard play here is to raise to build the pot and take the initiative. A 3x big blind raise to 300 is a standard sizing that should either win the blinds or set up a strong pot position against the blinds if they call.

Pocket fours are a strong speculative hand, and calling here gives me good implied odds to hit a set against the button's opening range. While playing out of position is a disadvantage, 3-betting would bloat the pot and force me to play a potentially large pot with a hand that is difficult to navigate post-flop if I don't improve. Calling is the most disciplined approach to see the flop.

I have pocket 3s in the Big Blind facing a button raise and a small blind call. I only need to call 200 chips to win a pot of 700. The true value of this hand lies in the implied odds. Both opponents have over 8,000 chips behind, which provides well over the required implied odds needed to profitably set-mine. Since I am closing the pre-flop action, there is no risk of a squeeze behind me. Calling to try and hit a set on the flop is a highly profitable play.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket fours on an A-7-3 rainbow board. My pair of fours is very weak here, as the Ace on the board hits the likely opening range of the Button and the Big Blind quite hard. There is no value in betting into two opponents, and I have no real draw. Checking is the only standard play to re-evaluate based on the action from my opponents.

I flopped bottom set on a very dry Ace-high rainbow board. With the pre-flop raiser (Flash) acting behind me on the Button, checking is the optimal play. The Ace heavily favors Flash's pre-flop raising range, making a continuation bet extremely likely. Leading out might fold out his bluffs or marginal hands, while checking allows him to hang himself with a c-bet, which I can then check-raise or check-call to build the pot.

I have top pair with the best possible kicker (TPTK) on a very dry board of 3s 7d Ac. Pre-flop, I was the aggressor from the button. Both the Small Blind and Big Blind have checked to me on the flop. This board is unlikely to have improved my opponents' ranges significantly unless one of them is slow-playing a set (33 or 77), though Aces (which I have a strong blocker for) are also possible. I will bet for value and to protect my equity against random floats or backdoor draws. A bet of around 40-50% of the pot is appropriate here to get called by weaker Aces or mid-pocket pairs.

The board is A-7-3 rainbow, which hits the Button's opening range significantly. My pocket fours are essentially bottom pair here and provide almost zero equity against the Button's range, which likely contains several Aces or high-card combinations that beat me. Calling a 400-chip bet into a 1300-chip pot with such a weak hand out of position against a player who already showed strength pre-flop is mathematically incorrect and will result in losing more chips on future streets. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play.

I flopped bottom set on a very dry A-7-3 rainbow board. Flash has continuation bet 400 into a pot of 900. Because the board is so dry, a check-raise here would look incredibly strong and likely force Flash to fold all of his bluffs and even some marginal made hands. By just calling, I conceal the strength of my hand, keep his entire range in the pot, and give him the opportunity to fire a second barrel on the turn, at which point I can look to raise and play for stacks.

The turn brings the Queen of Hearts, completing a perfectly dry rainbow board. This is a fantastic card for my bottom set because it hits Flash's perceived range extremely well. Hands like AQ just improved to top two pair, and he will very likely continue betting his strong Aces (AK, AJ) as well as broadway hands that picked up a gutshot or pair. By checking, I allow him to confidently fire a second barrel. This sets up a highly profitable check-raise to bloat the pot and play for his stack.

I have top pair with the best kicker (TPTK) on a board of A-7-3-Q. On the flop, I bet 400 and was called by Pro in the Big Blind. The Queen on the turn is a bit of a connector, but it is unlikely to have improved Pro's range more than mine, as I hold the Ace and King. Pro's check on the turn indicates they are likely in check-call mode with a weaker Ace or a middle pair. I should bet for value to get called by hands like AJ, AT, or potentially a Queen that just paired up. A bet of around half the pot (850) is a standard value sizing here to build the pot while keeping their calling range wide.

I checked the turn to allow Flash to fire a second barrel, and he has obliged with an 850 bet. The Queen is an excellent card for my bottom set, as it connects heavily with his pre-flop raising and flop c-betting range, meaning he often holds a strong Ace (AK, AJ) or just improved to two pair (AQ). Now is the ideal time to spring the trap and check-raise. Raising to 2600 builds the pot significantly and perfectly sets up an all-in shove on the river, while still giving his strong hands a price they will likely call.

I have Ace-King for top pair, top kicker (TPTK) on a relatively dry board. Pro's check-raise on the turn is a very strong move, typically representing a set of 3s or 7s, or two pair with AQ or A7. However, in a three-handed game, ranges are wider, and folding TPTK at this stage would be overly exploitable. I am getting roughly 3:1 pot odds to call. Raising further would likely only be called by hands that beat me, so I will call the raise to see the river and re-evaluate, keeping the pot from ballooning further if I am indeed behind.

The river brings a second Queen, which completely changes the landscape of the hand. While my plan on the turn was to shove the river, the Queen of Diamonds is arguably the worst card in the deck for my bottom set. Flash's call of my massive turn check-raise heavily weights his range towards strong made hands like AQ, AK, AA, or 77. The river Queen now improves his AQ to a superior full house (Queens full of Aces), meaning I am losing to a huge portion of his continuing range (AQ, AA, 77). If I shove here, I am value-owning myself: I will only get called by better full houses, while hands I actually beat (like AK or AJ) will almost certainly fold their two pair (Aces and Queens) to a river jam. Since I cannot get called by worse, betting has zero value. Checking is the optimal play to exercise pot control. If he checks behind, I win against his strong Aces; if he shoves, I can safely fold knowing he has me beat.

I hold Ace-King for two pair (Aces and Queens) with a King kicker on an A-7-3-Q-Q board. In the previous street (the turn), Pro check-raised me significantly, which usually signals a very strong hand like a set (33, 77), two pair (AQ, A7), or a semi-bluff. The river pairing the Queen is a very interesting card; it counterfeits hands like A7 and A3, but it also completes a full house for any hand containing a Queen or a pocket pair like 77 or 33. Pro's check on the river after a turn check-raise is polarizing—it could be a trap with a full house, or it could be a realization that their turn value-raise (with something like A7) is now less certain. Given the pot size and the strength of Pro's turn action, there is very little value in betting here. I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I don't want to face a check-raise from a monster. Checking back allows me to see the showdown and win against bluffs or weaker Aces.

6s 8c is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. There is no strategic value in playing this hand from the button, as it has poor equity against any calling or raising range. Folding is the most profitable play to preserve the stack.

I have J2o in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand. The Button has folded, and even though it is blind vs blind, playing such a weak offsuit hand out of position is unprofitable over the long run. I will simply fold and save my chips.
We are on the Button with Ks2s. In a 3-handed game, opening any suited King from the Button is standard strategy to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I'll raise to 2.5x the big blind.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 5d, a very weak hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. 85o does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against the chip leader. There is no reason to defend this hand or attempt a bluff at this stage. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

Holding 8-2 suited in the big blind against a standard button open is a clear fold. The hand lacks sufficient equity to defend profitably against a raising range, and there is no compelling reason to bloat the pot with a weak, disconnected hand out of position.

I am on the button with 9-8 offsuit. In a 3-handed game, this is a standard hand to open-raise with to apply pressure to the blinds and take control of the pot. A 3x raise to 300 is a standard sizing that allows me to win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position if called.

I am in the small blind with 7-4 suited. This is a weak starting hand and I am playing out of position against the button's opening raise. Calling would require committing 250 chips to an unfavorable situation where I will be at a disadvantage for the remainder of the hand. Folding is the most disciplined and strategically sound decision.

In a 3-handed game, the button's opening range is quite wide, but a 3x raise (to 300) combined with being out of position makes K4s a marginal to weak defense. Playing dominated Kings out of position can lead to difficult post-flop spots, especially when facing a relatively aggressive opponent. With a comfortable chip lead, there's no need to force the action with weak suited hands. Folding is the most solid play.

My hand, 2d 8s, is extremely weak and has very poor playability. In a three-handed game, while playing from the button, opening with such a bottom-tier holding is strategically unsound. Conserving my stack by folding is the prudent decision here.

I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak hand, 8c 5d (offsuit 8-high). The Button folded. Although I could attempt a steal from the Small Blind, the hand has almost no playability post-flop and the Big Blind could easily defend or re-steal. The most profitable long-term play with this hand is to simply fold and save my chips for better spots.
I have Ace-King suited (AsKs) on the Button, which is a premium starting hand. I will open-raise to build the pot and take control of the hand.

Ace-Queen offsuit is a premium starting hand in three-handed play. Since the Button has opened the action, I will three-bet to 850. This allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, or build a value pot while being out of position against the Button's wide opening range.

I am holding 8d 6d in the Big Blind, facing an open raise from the button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind. This is a speculative hand that is not strong enough to call a 3-bet out of position, especially against two opponents. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind, I hold a premium hand with AsKs. While calling in position is a viable option, 4-betting puts maximum pressure on Flash's roughly 54BB stack, allowing me to extract value from worse hands like AQ or mid-to-high pocket pairs, and gives me the initiative. I will 4-bet to 2200 to either win the pot pre-flop, deny equity, or induce an all-in shove which I am happily priced in to call.

In 3-handed play, Ace-Queen offsuit is a top-tier hand. Pro's button opening range is wide, and while a 4-bet is a sign of strength, folding AQo at this stack depth (approximately 45 BBs) would be overly conservative and exploitable. Given that calling the 2,200 raise would leave me with only 2,375 chips in a 4,500 chip pot, I am effectively pot-committed to any flop. By shoving all-in now, I maximize my fold equity against Pro's potential bluffs or medium pairs (like 77-99) and ensure I realize my full equity with two overcards to most non-pair hands.

Flash's 5-bet to 4575 leaves them with only 850 chips behind, meaning they are completely pot-committed. Holding AsKs, a premium hand, I am more than happy to get all the chips in pre-flop against their 5-bet range. Raising to 5425 puts Flash all-in, realizing our full equity and avoiding any post-flop decisions.

I am holding the King of hearts, which gives me the second-nut flush draw on a flop of Queen-Six-Jack of hearts. Pro has lead out with a small bet of 200 into a 600 pot (1/3 pot size). I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call, and my equity with a flush draw alone is approximately 35% to hit by the river. Given the favorable odds and the strength of the draw, calling is the best play to realize my equity while in position.

On the turn, the 3d is a blank. I have top pair and a ten-high flush draw on a monotone board. Checking here allows me to control the pot size out of position with a marginal made hand. If my opponent bets, my hand has enough showdown value and redraw equity to consider a call. If they check back, I get a free card to potentially improve to a flush or reach showdown cheaply.

I hold the King of hearts on a monochromatic flop (Qh-6h-Jh), giving me the second-nut flush draw. Pro has checked the turn after c-betting the flop. Since I am in position, checking behind allows me to realize my equity and see the river for free. I avoid the risk of a check-raise and can comfortably value bet if a heart arrives on the river.

The river puts four hearts on the board, completing my flush with the Ten of Hearts. With the Queen and Jack of hearts already out there, only the Ace and King of hearts beat me. Since the turn checked through, my opponent is less likely to have a premium heart that they would want to build the pot with. Betting half-pot here allows me to extract value from lower flushes (like the 9h, 8h, or 7h) while controlling the size of the pot in case my opponent happens to hold the nut or second-nut flush and decides to raise.

On a board with four hearts (Qh-6h-Jh-3d-4h), I hold the King of hearts, giving me the second-nut flush. The only hand that beats me is the Ace of hearts. Pro raised pre-flop from the Small Blind, c-bet the flop, and then checked the turn before leading for 500 on the river. This line could represent a variety of hands, including a smaller flush or a high card with a single heart. Since I hold the second-best possible hand, I must raise for value. A raise to 1500 is a standard size to get called by lower flushes while maximizing profit.

My opponent raises my river bet on a four-heart board. I hold the Ten of Hearts, giving me the third-nut flush (beaten only by the Ace and King of Hearts). Flash's turn check makes it somewhat less likely he flopped a monster, though he could easily have a hand like AhXx or KhXx that rivered a flush. However, he could also be bluffing or overvaluing a lower flush like the 9h or 8h. Given the pot odds, my third-nut flush is too strong to fold to a single raise. I will call to see the showdown.

With J8s on the Button in a 3-handed game, I have a solid suited one-gapper that plays well post-flop and has good steal equity pre-flop. I'll open with a standard raise to 250 to pressure the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Five offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. Queen-Five is a weak hand to play out of position against a raise, especially with a short-stacked player in the Big Blind who could potentially squeeze. Given that my stack is significantly larger than both opponents, I do not need to take marginal risks with dominated hands. The standard play here is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 10s-9c. Pro has opened to 250 on the button, and Flash has folded. The pot currently contains 400, and it costs me 150 to call. The pot odds are favorable to defend, and 10s-9c is a playable suited-connector that performs reasonably well in multi-street play. Given the stack sizes and the position, flatting is the most balanced and efficient way to proceed.

The board texture is highly favorable for the button's range, featuring both an Ace and a King. With only ten-high and a gutshot straight draw, I have very little showdown value and no reason to lead out. Checking is the most prudent strategy to control the size of the pot and evaluate the opponent's action.

On an A-K-8 rainbow board, I hit bottom pair with Jd 8d. This board heavily favors my pre-flop raising range, but my specific hand has marginal showdown value and little room to improve. A continuation bet here is likely to be called by better hands (Aces, Kings, or better Eights) and fold out worse ones. Checking behind for pot control allows me to realize my equity safely and evaluate on the turn.

I am holding Ten-high on a board of Ad-Kh-8s-3d. I checked the flop and the button checked behind, which suggests neither of us has a strong piece of this board. Betting here as a bluff into an Ace and King high board is unlikely to generate folds from any reasonable calling range. Checking is the most rational play to keep the pot small and potentially reach a cheap showdown.

The turn 3d gives me a diamond flush draw to go with my bottom pair of eights. Since Flashlight has checked twice, their range appears capped or heavily weighted toward weak showdown value or missed broadway hands. Betting now serves as a strong semi-bluff: it can fold out better pocket pairs or weak kings, while building the pot in case I hit my flush. I will bet around half pot to apply pressure and capitalize on my improved equity.

I am holding Ten-high with no pair, no draw, and essentially zero showdown value on a board of Ad-Kh-8s-3d. Pro checked the flop, but their turn bet indicates they are likely trying to take the pot down or they have at least a marginal hand like a weak Ace or King that they decided to delay c-betting. With no equity to improve and a board that heavily favors the button's range, calling is not profitable. Folding is the correct decision to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 9c 2c. While suited, this is a very weak hand. I have a large chip lead over both opponents (18,150 vs. ~6,000 each). However, playing a weak hand out of position post-flop or inducing a three-bet from the short stacks is unnecessary. Standard strategy in a 3-handed game allows for a wider opening range from the button, but 92s is generally a fold in most GTO and exploitative ranges unless the blinds are extremely tight. Given the stack dynamics, I will preserve my lead and fold.

The button folded, leaving me in the small blind with King-high offsuit. Given the low cost of calling to see a flop (50 chips) against the big blind, it is strategically sound to complete the blind to see if I can hit top pair or a favourable board. I will call to keep my range wide and see a flop.

With King-Queen suited, I have a premium hand, especially heads-up and in position against the Small Blind. Facing a limp, I want to raise to build the pot, take control of the initiative, and punish weak limps. A raise to 3bb (300) is standard here to force the SB to pay to see a flop or fold pre-flop.

The big blind has raised, and I am out of position with a marginal hand (King-Seven offsuit). Calling the raise would require committing more chips to a weak holding in a disadvantageous position, leading to difficult post-flop decisions. Folding is the most disciplined and strategically sound choice to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

King-Queen offsuit is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Being on the button with initiative, I should open the pot to build the size and potentially take down the blinds. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing to accomplish this.

Queen-deuce offsuit is an incredibly weak hand with essentially zero playability. Against a button raise to 3 big blinds, the standard and most profitable play is to fold immediately and save our chips.

I have Ace-Trey offsuit in the Big Blind. Flashlight opened to 300 from the Button, and Pro folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me 2.25:1 odds. Against a standard button opening range, an Ace-high hand is usually strong enough to defend, especially when getting reasonable pot odds. I will call to see a flop and play the hand from there, as 3-betting might be too risky against Flashlight's stack size and I don't want to fold a hand with this much equity against a steal attempt.

On a flop of 6s-Tc-7c, I have Ace-high with the Trey of clubs. This board is quite coordinated and likely hits a button opening range well. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard and most prudent play is to check to the raiser and see how they respond. I do not have enough equity to lead (donk bet) into this pot, and checking allows me to evaluate Flashlight's betting size and frequency before deciding whether to continue with my Ace-high.

The flop of 6s-Tc-7c is somewhat coordinated and hits a calling range, but I am the pre-flop raiser and have two overcards with King-Queen. Betting is the standard play here to maintain initiative and potentially take down the pot immediately, as my opponent checked to me. A continuation bet of 350 into the 650 pot is a solid size to balance value and protection while testing my opponent's strength.

On the flop of 6s-Tc-7c, I have Ace-high. This board is very wet and favors the pre-flop raiser's range more than mine. After checking, Flashlight bet 350 into a pot of 650. I have the Ace of diamonds and Trey of clubs, giving me a backdoor flush draw and two overcards, but this texture hits many hands in a button's range (78, 89, 9T, any two clubs, etc.). While I have Ace-high, calling here with only one overcard (the Ace) and no strong draw on such a connected board is risky, especially given Flashlight's relatively short stack (4975). However, getting nearly 3:1 on a call, I could potentially peeling one street if I think my Ace is live or if I pick up equity on the turn. But in a tournament setting with this board texture, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead, as I am out of position with very little hand strength.

I am on the Button in a 3-handed game and first to act pre-flop. My hand, Q2o, is extremely weak and ranks among the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. There is no strategic justification for entering the pot with this holding. Folding is the optimal and standard play.

I am in the Small Blind with KJs, which is a very strong hand three-handed. The button folded, and I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, or I can raise to take the initiative against Flashlight in the Big Blind. Given the stack sizes (around 60BB each), KJs is definitely a hand I want to play aggressively to potentially isolate or win the pot immediately. Raising here is standard for a professional player to put pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the big blind with 7-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Given that I have no equity to justify calling a raise with such a poor hand, I will fold to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with 83o. This is a very weak hand. While I have the largest stack at the table, there is no need to play trash hands like this from the button when both blinds are relatively short (around 60 big blinds). Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

I am in the small blind with 8d 9s. The button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. The pot is 150, and I need to call 50 more to see the flop. Given the pot odds of 3:1 and my relative stack size, calling to see a cheap flop is a reasonable, low-risk speculative play with a suited-connector-adjacent hand.
I am in the big blind with an extremely weak hand (4c 2h). The small blind just limped in. There is no reason to raise and bloat the pot with such poor cards, so I will check to see a free flop.

I am in the small blind with 8d 9s on a coordinated 7h 8h 6h board. I have a pair of 8s and an open-ended straight draw (5 or 10 makes a straight). The board is extremely wet with a flush draw. Since I am first to act and the pot is small (200), a bet of 150 is appropriate to define the strength of my hand and potentially charge for the draws, or to take down the pot if the opponent is weak.

I hold 4c 2h on a 7h 8h 6h monotone flop. I have essentially no equity, only a weak gutshot straight draw and the worst possible heart for a flush draw. With my opponent leading out, I have no reason to continue in this hand and must fold.
Ace-Jack offsuit is a strong hand to open with from the button. I want to build the pot and take the initiative against the blinds.

76o is too weak to play from the Small Blind against a Button raise. We will be out of position for the rest of the hand and 76o lacks the necessary playability or equity to justify a call or a 3-bet. Folding is the optimal decision.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s 7c, a weak suited-gapper. Flashlight on the Button has raised to 250, and Pro has folded from the Small Blind. The cost to call is 150 more into a pot of 400. Flashlight's stack is quite short (5725 chips, which is around 57 Big Blinds). Although the pot odds are roughly 2.6 to 1, 75o is not a hand that plays well out of position against a range that usually consists of many better high cards and pairs. Conserving chips for better spots is more efficient given my healthy stack lead.

With pocket threes on the Button, I have a decent hand to open with in position. A standard raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) can steal the blinds or let me see a flop heads-up with positional advantage, where I can set-mine or c-bet favorable boards.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-4 offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250. This is a very weak hand to defend with, especially against a Button raise that usually represents some range of strength or a late-position steal attempt. My stack is very healthy at 17,800, but there is no reason to waste chips calling out of position with a marginal hand like 8-4 offsuit. The correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

QJ offsuit is a strong hand for defending the big blind against a button open. I am getting favorable pot odds of 2.66:1 (150 to call into a 400 pot), making a call the most sound decision. I will see the flop and re-evaluate my position.

I am in the Big Blind out of position against the button. With Q-J on a Ten-Ten-Deuce paired board, I have no pair and no real draw. Checking is the most appropriate action to evaluate the opponent's strategy without committing further chips with a weak hand.

On a paired board of Th Td 2d, we have a marginal made hand with pocket 3s that is vulnerable to many overcards on the turn and river. A small continuation bet of around 1/3 to 40% of the pot works well here to fold out random overcards (like KQ or QJ) that have 6 outs against us, denying them free equity. It also helps define Flashlight's range; if we get check-raised, we can comfortably fold knowing we're likely beat by a Ten, a better pocket pair, or a strong flush draw.

The board is paired (Ten-Ten-Deuce), which is a difficult texture to navigate with only Queen-Jack high. Since I have no pair and no significant draw, and the opponent is representing strength with a continuation bet on this paired board, the most disciplined play is to fold rather than chase marginal equity against a potentially strong range.

I am on the button with 9h6h, a decent suited semi-connector. The current stack sizes are quite shallow for my opponents, Flashlight (5825) and Pro (6225), while I have a healthy lead with 17800. Raising here is a standard play to put pressure on the blinds and attempt to win the pot immediately or play a pot in position with a hand that has good post-flop playability. A 2.5x or 3x open is appropriate given the stack depths.

With JQo in the Small Blind, I am out of position against the Button's raise. While the hand has decent potential, playing OOP against a Button raise suggests a cautious approach. Calling the 200 to see a flop is the most balanced decision given the stack sizes and the pot odds, keeping the investment controlled.

With T9o in the Big Blind facing a button raise and a small blind call, we are out of position against the original raiser and our hand lacks the suitedness to play well multiway. Despite getting decent pot odds (150 to win 600), T9o has poor equity realization in this spot and is easily dominated by the broadway hands our opponents might be playing. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser on a King-high board that completely missed my Jack-Queen offsuit. With no pairs, no flush draw, and only a weak backdoor gutshot, I have no reason to continue if Flash bets. Checking is the standard play here to see if the opponent bets, and I will comfortably fold to any significant aggression.

I raised pre-flop with 9h6h and was called by the Small Blind. The flop comes King-high (Kh 8d 5h), which generally favors my perceived range as the initial raiser. Although I only have nine-high, I have a backdoor flush draw and the board is disconnected enough that a continuation bet is likely to succeed against a wide range of hands that missed. Flashlight checked to me, so I will bet 300 (half pot) to maintain initiative and attempt to take the pot down now.

I have completely missed this flop with my Jack-high hand. The board is King-high, which heavily favors the aggressor's range. Without even a backdoor draw or a pair, I have zero equity to justify calling a bet of 300 into a 900 pot. Folding is the only sensible decision here to preserve my stack.

7h Jd is a weak starting hand with little showdown value or potential to connect strongly with the board. Given that I am on the button and looking to preserve my stack for a more advantageous spot, folding is the most disciplined and optimal play here.

97s is a very playable suited one-gapper. From the small blind, raising to 300 puts pressure on the big blind to defend, and gives us the betting initiative heading into the flop if called. Flash has shown a willingness to fold the big blind to SB opens, making this a profitable steal with good playability post-flop.

I am currently the chip leader with a significant margin. Pro (Small Blind) has raised to 300, and I am in the Big Blind holding King-Deuce offsuit. While I have position on Pro and am getting 2:1 pot odds to call, K2o is a very weak hand that doesn't play well post-flop, especially against a potentially strong range from Pro who has been active. Given my dominant stack and the tournament progress (hand 81 of 150), I prefer to avoid unnecessary variance and marginal spots against the other significant stack. I will fold and wait for better opportunities to leverage my chip lead.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong hand to open from the Button. It plays well in position, blocks premium hands, and has good fold equity against the blinds. Raising to 2.5x (250 chips) is a standard open size to apply pressure while managing risk.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Nine offsuit. Pro (Button) has raised to 250, which is a standard opening size from that position. With a significant chip lead over both opponents, I have the flexibility to play hands with decent potential. King-Nine is strong enough to defend the small blind against a button open, especially getting 2.75-to-1 odds on a call. While I could 3-bet to exert pressure, calling allows me to see a flop and use my stack advantage post-flop without bloating the pot out of position with a marginal hand.

Q3o is a weak hand with poor potential in a multi-way pot. Despite the decent pot odds, the positional disadvantage and the low strength of the hand make folding the most disciplined and profitable decision to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

On this flop of Td-Js-3s, I have King-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen) and a backdoor spade draw. The button (Pro) raised pre-flop, and this board texture hits a lot of a button opening range (J-x, T-x, draws). Since I am out of position, checking to the initial raiser is the standard play. I will evaluate based on his bet size if he chooses to continuation bet.

I have middle pair with top kicker on a wet board (Td Js 3s). This board connects well with the Small Blind's pre-flop calling range, which includes many straight draws, flush draws, and hands containing a Jack. By checking behind, I can control the pot size with my marginal made hand, avoid facing a difficult check-raise, and evaluate the turn card in position.

On the turn, the board now shows three spades (Js, 3s, 5s) and I hold the King of Spades, giving me the second-nut flush draw. The flop went check-check after Pro's pre-flop raise, which suggests Pro likely does not have a monster hand. By checking here, I am looking to realize my equity and potentially see a free river card or call a reasonably sized bet. If a fourth spade arrives on the river, I will likely have the best hand, and if not, I still have some showdown value with King-high or a potential straight if a Queen hits. Leading out now might force a fold from hands I'm ahead of or get raised by a made flush, so checking is the most consistent play.

The turn card brings a third spade to the board, completing some possible flushes. I have middle pair with top kicker and the Ten of Spades, giving me a marginal made hand with a drawing component. Since Flash has checked again, my hand has decent showdown value but is rarely ahead of hands that would call a substantial bet, nor is it strong enough to confidently face a check-raise. Checking behind ensures pot control, realizes my equity for free, and allows me to evaluate the river in position.

On the river, the King of Clubs gives me top pair with my King of Spades. The board shows three spades, and while I hold the second-nut flush blocker (Ks), the flush did not complete on the river. Pro (the initial pre-flop raiser) has checked both the flop and the turn, which strongly suggests a capped or weak range, likely consisting of marginal made hands like a Jack or a Ten, or missed overcards. By betting 400 into a 600-chip pot, I am aiming to extract value from those J-x or T-x hands that would likely check behind if I were to check. While a straight (AQ or Q9) is possible, Pro's passive line makes it less likely than a weak one-pair hand.

The river brings a King, placing a second overcard to my pair of Tens on a highly coordinated board (Td Js 3s 5s Kc). Facing a 2/3 pot bet from Flash after the action checked down, my third pair is reduced to a pure bluff catcher. While Flash could be stabbing with missed draws, my hand (Ah Ts) blocks some of his potential bluffs like AQ, and he has plenty of King-x, Jack-x, or better hands in his range that he might value bet after I showed weakness twice. Folding is the safest and most disciplined choice.

I am on the button with a decent hand, Jack-Ten offsuit. With my significant chip lead (nearly 3x my opponents' stacks), I should be applying pressure to the shorter stacks in the blinds. JTo is a strong raising hand in a 3-handed game from the button. Raising to 250 (2.5x BB) is a standard open that allows me to take control of the pot early and potentially win the blinds or see a flop in position.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Five suited (As5s), which is a solid hand in a short-handed game. Flash has opened from the button, and calling is the most balanced approach here. It allows me to see a flop with good equity without overcommitting to a 3-bet, especially considering I'm out of position. The call amount of 200 chips (to match the 250 total) is well within my stack size and offers decent pot odds.

I am in the Big Blind with Q8o, a weak offsuit hand. Facing a button raise and a call from the small blind, playing this hand out of position multiway will be unprofitable. The hand lacks strong high-card value, playability, or drawing potential, making a fold the clear choice.

The board texture is extremely wet with three clubs, making a flush possible immediately. While I have two pair (Aces and Fives), it is very vulnerable against any single club in my opponent's range. Checking is the most prudent action to control the pot size and see how Flash, the pre-flop aggressor, reacts to this dangerous flop. I will look to evaluate further based on their action.

As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a significant range advantage on this Ace-high monotone board, as my range contains many more Ax hands and high flush combinations than the small blind's calling range. Although I have completely missed with Jd Tc, a continuation bet is standard here to capitalize on that range advantage and fold out the opponent's air or small pocket pairs without a club. I will bet 300, which is 50% of the pot, to apply pressure while maintaining a healthy stack lead.

The flop is highly coordinated with three clubs, making any single club in Flash's range a significant threat. While I have two pair with Aces and Fives, it is a vulnerable holding. Flash has bet 300 into a 900 pot. With pot odds of 3:1, calling is the most balanced approach. It allows me to see the Turn card without overcommitting, while keeping the pot size manageable. Raising here is too aggressive on such a wet board where I could easily be dominated by a flush.

The board texture is slightly improved by the 9 of diamonds, which is not a club, reducing the immediate threat of a completed flush draw compared to the flop, though a made flush remains a significant threat. Holding two pair (Aces and Fives), I have a hand with decent showdown value but it is vulnerable to better hands or future club draws. Checking is the most prudent strategy OOP (out of position) to control the pot size. It allows me to see if Flash checks behind, granting me a free river, or bets, allowing me to re-evaluate based on the sizing and pot odds.

After Flashlight called my flop continuation bet on this monotone Ace-high board, their range is likely narrowed to made hands like an Ace or a flush, or a strong draw with a high club. The 9d turn is a brick that doesn't improve my hand. Betting again would risk a significant portion of my stack against a range that is unlikely to fold. I will check back to keep the pot small, exercise pot control, and maintain my dominant chip lead while seeing the river for free.

On the river, the board is [5c, 2c, Ac, 9d, 2s]. I hold [5s, As], giving me two pair (Aces and Fives). This is a strong hand that beats any single Ace, which is a significant part of the opponent's range. However, I lose to any pocket pair of 2s or any 2 in their hand, which makes trips. Given that the turn was checked through, it is unlikely the opponent has a monster hand like a full house, but a 2 is definitely in their potential range. Checking is the most balanced approach as it allows me to induce a bluff from a worse hand while minimizing losses if I am beat by a 2. Betting might only be called by hands that have me beat or fold hands that I already beat.

Flashlight checked both the turn and the river after calling a flop bet on an Ace-high monotone board. This sequence often indicates a marginal made hand like a weak Ace or a middle pocket pair that is looking to reach a cheap showdown. My Jack-high has virtually no showdown value, but bluffing into a capped but potentially sticky range is risky given my substantial chip lead. I will check back, conceding the pot if necessary, to preserve my stack and continue my strategy of low-variance play while maintaining a dominant position in the tournament.

K-J offsuit is a strong hand to open on the button in a 3-handed game. Raising to 250, which is 2.5 times the big blind, is a standard opening size to claim initiative, potentially steal the blinds, and play the hand with the positional advantage.

K4s is a marginal suited hand. Facing a raise from the Button while in the Small Blind, playing out of position with the Big Blind still to act is unprofitable. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

I am in the Big Blind with T4o. Flashlight has raised to 250 from the Button. While T4o has some high-card value against a wide button opening range, it plays very poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given my large stack advantage over both opponents (I have nearly 3x their stacks), I don't need to take marginal spots or defend with weak hands like this. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to apply pressure.

K2s is a marginal hand even on the Button in a 3-handed game. With a weak kicker and relatively active opponents in the blinds, folding is a solid, low-variance play to preserve chips for better spots.

I have been dealt Ace-King offsuit (AKo), which is a premier starting hand. Since the Button has folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind. With a significant chip lead over my remaining opponent, I want to take control of the hand and build a pot immediately. A standard raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate here to extract value and potentially take the blinds down.

I am defending the big blind with Jack-four suited against the small blind's raise. The pot odds of 2:1 (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600) justify a call in this position, especially given my stack depth. I will see a flop and evaluate from there.

I have flopped top pair with the best possible kicker (AKo) on a board of K-5-3. This is a very strong hand. I need to bet for value against weaker Kings (KQ, KJ, KT), pocket pairs (66-QQ), or potential spade draws. A bet of 400 into a 600 pot is appropriate to build the pot and charge draws.

I am in the big blind with Jack-four suited on a King-high board. My hand has very little equity against the opponent's betting range, and I do not have a strong enough draw to justify continuing. The bet of 400 into a 600 pot is significant, and since I am likely behind, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with 5h 2d, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given the current stack dynamics where I have a significant chip lead over Flashlight and Pro, there is no strategic reason to open-raise with junk. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind against the Big Blind (Pro). With 6-10 offsuit, the hand is marginal, but given that the button has folded, I am heads-up. The pot is 150 and I only need to call 50 to see a flop, offering 3:1 pot odds. Completing the small blind is a standard, low-risk play here.

With King-Nine offsuit in the Big Blind against a Small Blind limper, I have a decent marginal hand. Since I'm in position post-flop, I can comfortably check to see a free flop rather than bloating the pot with an iso-raise. This keeps variance lower while still allowing me to realize my equity and leverage positional advantage.

I hold two pair (Tens and Sixes) on an Ace-high board. While this is a decent hand, it is vulnerable to any Ace in the Big Blind's range. Since I am in the Small Blind acting first, checking is the standard play to control the pot size, see how the opponent reacts, and avoid bloating the pot against a potentially stronger hand like an Ace.

With King-Nine offsuit on an Ace-Ten-Six board, I have no pair and no strong draw. The Small Blind limped pre-flop and checked the flop, which could mean a weak made hand (like a weak Ace or Ten) or a complete miss. Betting here as a bluff is unnecessary and risky given the board texture. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, take a free card, and rely on my positional advantage on later streets.

I hold two pair (Tens and Sixes) on a board of Ace-Ten-Six-Deuce. My hand is strong, and the fact that both players checked the flop suggests the opponent likely does not have a strong Ace. I am betting to get value from weaker holdings like a lower pair, a ten, or a draw.

On the turn, facing a bet of 150 from the Small Blind on an Ace-Ten-Six-Deuce board, I only have King high with no viable draws. Calling here is unprofitable as the opponent is likely betting a made hand like a weak Ace, a Ten, or even a Six. Raising as a bluff is also unnecessary and risky given the board texture and my lack of equity. Folding is the only sensible play.

Holding Ace-Five suited on the button is a strong starting hand, offering good equity and positional advantage. I will open the pot with a standard raise to 250 to potentially steal the blinds or play a hand in position against the small or big blind.
I am in the Small Blind with an offsuit 9-7, which is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from the Button, the most profitable and standard play is to fold.

I am holding 2s 3c in the big blind. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While I have a significant chip lead and the pot odds to call are roughly 2.6:1, playing such a weak hand out of position against the button raiser (Flashlight) is not profitable in the long run. If he bets, I have a decision to make, but I avoid over-committing with a weak kicker on a board where the flush just got home.

The opponent checked to me on the turn. I have a spade flush draw with my Jack of Spades, and the board shows three other spades, giving me a 4-flush. With King-high, I currently have no made hand to contest any Ace or better-made hand. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity and see the river for free without ballooning the pot with a drawing hand against a potential check-raise.

The river brings a fourth spade to the board (As 9s 7s 6s) and completes several straight possibilities (any 8 makes a straight). By checking, I can potentially see a cheap showdown or decide whether to call a bet if I suspect a bluff, though with four spades out, even a small spade in his hand beats me. Check is the safest play to control the pot.

The river is the Nine of Spades, completing a four-flush on the board. With my Jack of Spades, I have made an Ace-high flush. Checking behind secures the pot and moves to showdown, which is the most prudent play given my relative hand strength in a checked-down pot.

The river 3d completes the backdoor diamond draw and pairs the board. At this point, our Tens and Threes with a Jack kicker is purely a bluff-catcher.
